title: practical english usage - Part 84
content:
He's the oldest athlete ever to win an Olympic gold medal. (= ... who  has grammar •  102   infinitives after nouns and pronouns: my decision to leave Do you think we'll have (enough) time to do some shopping?

There's hardly (enough) room to breathe in here.

Enough is often dropped before room and time.

We've got plenty of time to see the British Museum.

There's too much  snow (for us) to be able to drive.

There was enough light to see what I was doing.

by noun + infinitive.

Quantifiers like enough, too much/many/little/few, plenty, etc are often followed 4 enough,        too    much,      etc The kids want somewhere to practise their music.

Some/any/nowhere can also be followed by infinitives.

He needs a place to live in. (     ... a-plaee-tedwe-inr-it:) not I gave her a paper to read, (     ... a paper to read it.) pronoun after the infinitive.

If the noun or pronoun is the object of the infinitive, we  do not add an object Did you tell her which bus to take? Is there anything to drink?

Is there any milk to put on the cornflakes?

I need some more books to read. (I will read the books.) The  noun or pronoun can also be like the object of the infinitive.

It was a war to end all wars. I'd like something to stop my toothache.

Have you got a key to open this door? (The key will open the door.) does with it. The  noun or pronoun can be like the subject of the infinitive.

to explain the purpose of a particular thing: what it does, or what somebody An infinitive can be used after a noun, or an indefinite pronoun like something, 3 purpose:     a  key    to   open      the    door It's a pleasure to see you again. (= It's pleasant to see you again.) What a nuisance to have to go! (= How  annoying to have to go!) You were ɑ  fool to agree. (= You were foolish to agree.) or which  have an adjectival sense.

We   can also use infinitives after some nouns which  are related to adjectives, 2 nouns     related      to  adjectives:       You     were      a  fool     to   agree.

a particular noun, ɪt is best to check  in a good dictionary.

Unfortunately, there is no easy way to decide which  structures are possible after I have no intention of returning.

- I do not intend to return.

1 understand her preference for living alone.

- She prefers to live alone.

There's no hope of arriving.

- I hope to arrive.

structures. Compare: And note that not all related verbs and nouns are followed by the same not I hate the thought of getting old. (     ... the thought to get old.) Not all nouns can be followed by infinitives in this way.

Is there any need to ask Jasmine? (= Do  we  need to ask Jasmine?) to leave.) I told her about my decision to leave. (= ɪ  told her that ɪ  had decided / have no wish to change. (= I do not wish to change.) -ing                                                         Section Infinitives,        forms     and   Past   Participles       after    Nouns,    Verbs,   etc         9 grammar •  103   -ing forms after nouns and adjectives: tired of listening I must find something to kill that fly.

we  are more likely to use an infinitive (►   468.2).

material. When  we  talk about somebody's purpose in using a particular object, This structure is mostly used to talk in general about types of object and I need something for killing flies.

Have you got any stuff for cleaning silver?

A strimmer is a machine for cutting grass and weeds.

is for.

something or anything, to explain the purpose of an object or material - what it For + -ing form can be used after a noun, or after an indefinite pronoun such as 2 purpose:     a  machine         for    cutting followed by -ing forms. It is best to check  in a good dictionary.

Unfortunately, there is no easy way to decide which  nouns and adjectives can be not She's very good at solving problems, (     . . . good to solve ...) not I'm tired of listening to this, (     I'm tired to listen . ..) not The thought of failing never entered his head, (     The thought tofaib...) not I hate the idea of getting old. (     .. .-the ideategetold.) (►   105.11,  105.13-105.16 for some exceptions with adjectives).
